```{r}
  annotate("segment", x = -20, xend = 20, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 20, xend = -20, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-2, 13), color = "grey30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-2, 13, -10, 0, 15), y = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
             size = 3, color = "black") + 
  annotate("text", x = -2, y = -0.5, label = "-2", size = 6) + 
  annotate("text", x = 13, y = -0.5, label = "13", size = 6) + 
  annotate("text", x = -10, y = -0.5, label = "-10", size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = 15, y = -0.5, label = "15", size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = -10, y = 0.5, label = "Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  annotate("text", x = 6, y = 0.5, label = "Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  annotate("text", x = 16, y = 0.5, label = "Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = -0.5, label = "0", size = 6) + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-20, 20), ylim = c(-0.75, 0.75)) +
  theme_void()
```

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $f\left(x\right)$

*Solution.* 

**Domain:** $\boxed{~\left(-\infty, -2\right]\cup \left[13, \infty\right)~}$

:::{.fragment}

**Roots:** We'll solve $f\left(x\right) = 0$

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)} &= 0
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $f\left(x\right)$

*Solution.* 

**Domain:** $\boxed{~\left(-\infty, -2\right]\cup \left[13, \infty\right)~}$

**Roots:** We'll solve $f\left(x\right) = 0$

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)} &= 0\\
\implies x^2 - 11x - 26 &= 0
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $f\left(x\right)$

*Solution.* 

**Domain:** $\boxed{~\left(-\infty, -2\right]\cup \left[13, \infty\right)~}$

**Roots:** We'll solve $f\left(x\right) = 0$

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)} &= 0\\
\implies x^2 - 11x - 26 &= 0\\
\implies \left(x - 13\right)\left(x + 2\right) &= 0
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $f\left(x\right)$

*Solution.* 

**Domain:** $\boxed{~\left(-\infty, -2\right]\cup \left[13, \infty\right)~}$

**Roots:** We'll solve $f\left(x\right) = 0$

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)} &= 0\\
\implies x^2 - 11x - 26 &= 0\\
\implies \left(x - 13\right)\left(x + 2\right) &= 0\\
\implies x = -2 &\text{ or } x = 13
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $f\left(x\right)$

*Solution.* 

**Domain:** $\boxed{~\left(-\infty, -2\right]\cup \left[13, \infty\right)~}$

**Roots:** The roots of $f\left(x\right)$ are at $\boxed{~\left(-2, 0\right)~}$ and $\boxed{~\left(13, 0\right)~}$

:::{.fragment}

**$y$-intercept:** Note that $x = 0$ is not in the domain of the function. This means that there is no $y$-intercept.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**End behavior:** We'll analyse $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)}}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)}}$

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = \lim_{x\to -\infty}{\sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.fragment}

The quadratic under the root goes off to infinity.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Because of this, the root goes off to infinity as well.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} &= \infty
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = \lim_{x\to \infty}{\sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.fragment}

The quadratic under the root goes off to infinity.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Because of this, the root goes off to infinity as well.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} &= \infty
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::



</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 11x - 26\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $f\left(x\right)$

*Solution.* 

**Domain:** $\boxed{~\left(-\infty, -2\right]\cup \left[13, \infty\right)~}$

**Roots:** The roots of $f\left(x\right)$ are at $\boxed{~\left(-2, 0\right)~}$ and $\boxed{~\left(13, 0\right)~}$

**$y$-intercept:** Note that $x = 0$ is not in the domain of the function. This means that there is no $y$-intercept.

**End behavior:** We've found $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = \infty}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = \infty}$

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig.height: 3.25

x1 <- seq(-10, -2, length.out = 500)
x2 <- seq(13, 20, length.out = 500)
f <- function(x) sqrt(x^2 - 11*x - 26)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x1, y = f(x1)), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x2, y = f(x2)), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-2, 13), y = c(0, 0)), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("segment", x = -10, xend = 20, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -1, yend = 12,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-10, 20), ylim = c(-1, 12)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-10, 20, by = 5)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = \\sqrt{x^2 - 11x - 26}$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Domain:** We'll start by finding the boundary values.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} 16 - x^2 &= 0
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** We'll start by finding the boundary values.

\begin{align} 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 &= 16
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** We'll start by finding the boundary values.

\begin{align} 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 &= 16\\
\implies x &= \pm 4
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.height: 1

ggplot() + 
  annotate("segment", x = -10, xend = 10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 10, xend = -10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-4, 4), color = "grey30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-4, 4), y = c(0, 0)),
             size = 3, color = "black") + 
  annotate("text", x = -4, y = -0.5, label = "-4", size = 6) + 
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -0.5, label = "4", size = 6) +
  #annotate("text", x = -5, y = 0.5, label = "(-)(-): Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  #annotate("text", x = 2, y = 0.5, label = "(-)(+): Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  #annotate("text", x = 9.5, y = 0.5, label = "(+)(+): Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = -0.5, label = "0", size = 6) + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-10, 10), ylim = c(-0.75, 0.75)) +
  theme_void()
```

:::

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** We'll start by finding the boundary values.

\begin{align} 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 &= 16\\
\implies x &= \pm 4
\end{align}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.height: 1

ggplot() + 
  annotate("segment", x = -10, xend = 10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 10, xend = -10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-4, 4), color = "grey30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-4, 4), y = c(0, 0)),
             size = 3, color = "black") + 
  annotate("text", x = -4, y = -0.5, label = "-4", size = 6) + 
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -0.5, label = "4", size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = -7, y = 0.5, label = "Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  #annotate("text", x = 2, y = 0.5, label = "(-)(+): Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  #annotate("text", x = 9.5, y = 0.5, label = "(+)(+): Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = -0.5, label = "0", size = 6) + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-10, 10), ylim = c(-0.75, 0.75)) +
  theme_void()
```

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** We'll start by finding the boundary values.

\begin{align} 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 &= 16\\
\implies x &= \pm 4
\end{align}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.height: 1

ggplot() + 
  annotate("segment", x = -10, xend = 10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 10, xend = -10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-4, 4), color = "grey30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-4, 4), y = c(0, 0)),
             size = 3, color = "black") + 
  annotate("text", x = -4, y = -0.5, label = "-4", size = 6) + 
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -0.5, label = "4", size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = -7, y = 0.5, label = "Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.5, label = "Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  #annotate("text", x = 9.5, y = 0.5, label = "(+)(+): Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = -0.5, label = "0", size = 6) + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-10, 10), ylim = c(-0.75, 0.75)) +
  theme_void()
```

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** We'll start by finding the boundary values.

\begin{align} 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 &= 16\\
\implies x &= \pm 4
\end{align}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.height: 1

ggplot() + 
  annotate("segment", x = -10, xend = 10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 10, xend = -10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-4, 4), color = "grey30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-4, 4), y = c(0, 0)),
             size = 3, color = "black") + 
  annotate("text", x = -4, y = -0.5, label = "-4", size = 6) + 
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -0.5, label = "4", size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = -7, y = 0.5, label = "Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.5, label = "Positive", size = 6, color = "darkgreen") +
  annotate("text", x = 7, y = 0.5, label = "Negative", size = 6, color = "darkred") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = -0.5, label = "0", size = 6) + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-10, 10), ylim = c(-0.75, 0.75)) +
  theme_void()
```

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

:::{.fragment}

**Roots:** We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)} &= 0
\end{align}

:::


</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)} &= 0\\
\implies 16 - x^2 &= 0
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)} &= 0\\
\implies 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 = 16
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$

\begin{align} \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)} &= 0\\
\implies 16 - x^2 &= 0\\
\implies x^2 = 16\\
\implies x = \pm 4
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

There's no surprise here. The roots are the boundary values we found earlier.

:::

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** The roots of $g\left(x\right)$ are at $\left(-4, 0 \right)$ and $\left(4, 0\right)$.

:::{.fragment}

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $g\left(0\right)$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - \left(0\right)^2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** The roots of $g\left(x\right)$ are at $\left(-4, 0 \right)$ and $\left(4, 0\right)$.

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $g\left(0\right)$.

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= \sqrt{\left(16 - \left(0\right)^2\right)}\\
&= \sqrt{16}
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** The roots of $g\left(x\right)$ are at $\left(-4, 0 \right)$ and $\left(4, 0\right)$.

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $g\left(0\right)$.

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= \sqrt{\left(16 - \left(0\right)^2\right)}\\
&= \sqrt{16}\\
&= 4
\end{align}

</div>

## Analysis of Radical Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** Find the domain, roots, $y$-intercept, and end behavior of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(16 - x^2\right)}}$. Use your results to sketch the graph of $g\left(x\right)$.

*Solution.*

**Domain:** $\left[-4, 4\right]$

**Roots:** The roots of $g\left(x\right)$ are at $\left(-4, 0 \right)$ and $\left(4, 0\right)$.

**$y$-intercept:** The $y$-intercept of the function is at $\left(0, 4\right)$.

:::{.fragment}

**End Behavior:** Not applicable since the domain does not extend toward positive or negative infinity.

:::

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig.height: 3.25

x_vals <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 500)
f <- function(x) sqrt(16 - x^2)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = f(x_vals)), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = c(-4, 4), y = c(0, 0)), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("segment", x = -6, xend = 6, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -1, yend = 6,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-6, 6), ylim = c(-1, 6)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-6, 6, by = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 6, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$g(x) = \\sqrt{16 - x^2}$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

</div>

## Radical Function Practice

<div style="font-size:16pt">

For each radical function below, find: (i) the domain, (ii) any roots, and (iii) the $y$-intercept (if one exists).

:::{.fragment}

**Try It! 1:** $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(4 - 2x\right)}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It! 2:** $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(3x^2 - 4x - 15\right)}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It! 3:** $\displaystyle{h\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 9\right)}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It! 4:** $\displaystyle{j\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 + 9\right)}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It! 5:** $\displaystyle{k\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x + 3\right)^2}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

> **Challenge for Try It! 5:** Before computing, predict what the graph will look like. Then simplify $\sqrt{\left(x+3\right)^2}$ algebraically. Does the result match your prediction?

:::

</div>

## Radical Functions and Inverse Functions

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

Radical functions arise naturally as *inverses of power functions*.

:::

:::{.fragment}

For example, $f\left(x\right) = x^2$ is not invertible over all of $\mathbb{R}$ — it fails the horizontal line test. But if we restrict its domain to $x \geq 0$, then $f\left(x\right) = x^2$ is invertible, and its inverse is $f^{-1}\left(x\right) = \sqrt{x}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

This is one justification for why $\sqrt{x}$ is only defined for $x \geq 0$. It's the inverse of a function whose restricted domain was $x \geq 0$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

More generally, if $n$ is even, then $f\left(x\right) = x^n$ restricted to $x \geq 0$ has inverse $f^{-1}\left(x\right) = x^{1/n} = \sqrt[n]{x}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

For *odd* $n$, no domain restriction is needed because $f\left(x\right) = x^n$ already passes the horizontal line test on all of $\mathbb{R}$.

:::

</div>

## Exit Ticket Task

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="75%"}

Navigate to [our MAT142 Exit Ticket Form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSeUo4K9WBH8xFAVjt3VbJAtkSMimu9DGD7Jv6zPLhZhifCIfQ/viewform?usp=dialog), answer the questions, and complete the *task* below.

<br/>

*Note.* Today's discussion is listed as `15. Radical Functions and Equations`

:::

:::{.column width="25%"}

![](MAT142_ExitTicket_QR.png)

:::

::::

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Task:** Consider $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \sqrt{\left(x^2 - 4x - 12\right)}}$.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the domain of $f\left(x\right)$ using sign testing.

$\left(b\right)$ Find any roots and the $y$-intercept (if one exists).

$\left(c\right)$ Sketch a rough graph.

</div>

## Summary and Next Time...

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.nonincremental}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

<center>**Ideas From Today**</center>

+ A **radical function** $f\left(x\right) = \left(g\left(x\right)\right)^{1/n}$ has domain that depends on whether $n$ is even or odd.
    + Odd index: all real numbers.
    + Even index: solve $g\left(x\right) \geq 0$ using sign testing.
+ **Sign testing:** find roots of $g$, test one point per interval, keep intervals where the sign is non-negative.
+ **Roots** of $f$ occur where $g\left(x\right) = 0$.
+ Radical functions arise as inverses of power functions with domain restrictions.
+ **Extraneous solutions** can appear whenever you multiply both sides by a variable expression (rational equations) or raise both sides to a power (radical equations) — always check solutions.

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

<center>**Looking Ahead**</center>

+ With algebraic functions complete, we move into **exponential and logarithmic functions**.
+ These are genuinely new classes of function. For example, exponential functions have the independent variable in the exponent.
+ The inverse relationship between exponentials and logarithms connects directly back to our Day 9 discussion on inverse functions.
    + Take a look back at that discussion for a refresher.

:::

::::

:::

:::{.fragment}

<center>**Next Time:** <br/> Basics of Exponentials and Logarithms</center>

:::

:::{.fragment}

<center>**Homework:** <br/> Complete Homework 10 on MyOpenMath</center>

:::

</div>
